steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Text 2 explains why Hurley's study (no predation relief effect on mule deer) differs from studies like Byrom's (predation relief effect on arctic ground squirrels). The author of Text 2 attributes the difference to prey characteristics: studies showing an increase in prey abundance "often focus on small, rapidly reproducing prey, like birds, squirrels, and rabbits," while Hurley's involved "large, slowly reproducing prey, like mule deer." We need the statement the Text 2 author would most likely agree with.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — They may be partially explained by the fact that lynxes are smaller and reproduce more rapidly than coyotes.: Text 2 never discusses predator body size or predator reproduction rates. The explanation given is about prey characteristics, not predator traits. ✗
  • Choice B — They may have been different if the ratio of the body size of lynxes to that of arctic ground squirrels was greater than it is.: Text 2 says nothing about predator-to-prey size ratios. This introduces a comparison Text 2 doesn't make. ✗
  • Choice C — They may reflect a response that does not tend to be observed in prey species other than arctic ground squirrels.: Text 2 explicitly says "many other" predation relief studies on small, rapidly reproducing prey (birds, rabbits, squirrels) show the same pattern as Byrom's. The response is not unique to arctic ground squirrels. ✗
  • Choice D — They may be partially attributable to physical characteristics of arctic ground squirrels that are not shared by mule deer.: Text 2 argues that prey type (small and rapidly reproducing vs. large and slowly reproducing) accounts for the difference in results. Arctic ground squirrels have characteristics—small size, rapid reproduction—that mule deer do not share, and Text 2 treats that as the key explanatory factor. ✓
That matches Choice D — They may be partially attributable to physical characteristics of arctic ground squirrels that are not shared by mule deer..
